personas.name: Rachel Torres
personas.background: Works from her studio in the Mission District. Clients include book publishers, game studios, and indie magazines. Studied at RISD. Lost 30% of her contracts to AI art in 2024.
personas.location_context: Member of the SF Illustrators Guild, organizing against AI art in commercial contracts

personas.name: Vikram Patel
personas.background: Building generative image models. PhD from Stanford in computer vision. Believes AI is a creative tool, not a replacement for artists.
personas.location_context: Works in SoMa, daily user and builder of AI creative tools

personas.name: Margaret "Maggie" Liu
personas.background: Partner at a tech-focused law firm in the Financial District. Has argued copyright cases at the 9th Circuit. Teaches IP law at Hastings.
personas.location_context: Advises both AI companies and creative clients on copyright

personas.name: DJ Smooth (Darius Johnson)
personas.background: Produces beats in his Bayview studio. Uses AI tools for sound design but hand-mixes everything. Released 3 albums. Sees both sides firsthand.
personas.location_context: Performs at venues across SF and Oakland, uses AI for sound design experiments

personas.name: Hannah Bergström
personas.background: Makes AI-assisted art on TikTok and Instagram. 200K followers. Uses Midjourney and custom-trained models as part of her creative process.
personas.location_context: Based in Hayes Valley, part of SF's digital creative community

key_facts: US Copyright Office ruled AI-generated images without human authorship are not copyrightable (Thaler v. Perlmutter)
AI image models were trained on billions of copyrighted works without permission
Freelance illustration rates dropped 25-40% in sectors where AI art is accepted
The Copyright Office allows registration for AI-assisted works where human creativity is substantial
supporting_evidence: Three class-action lawsuits pending against AI art companies for training on copyrighted data
concerns: Copyright for AI art devalues human creativity
Trained on stolen work — the entire foundation is tainted

sources.title: US Copyright Office AI guidance
sources.snippet: Works generated by AI without human authorship are not eligible for copyright registration.

sources.title: Illustrators Guild market impact survey
sources.snippet: 40% of surveyed illustrators reported lost contracts to AI-generated alternatives.

worldviews.stance_reasoning: You can't copyright something built from stolen work. AI models scraped my portfolio without asking. Now they want legal protection for the output? That's laundering plagiarism through an algorithm.

key_facts: Human-AI collaboration produces work neither could create alone
Prompt engineering and model fine-tuning require genuine creative decisions
Photography faced identical 'is it art?' debates in the 1880s
Most commercial AI art involves extensive human curation, editing, and iteration
supporting_evidence: Photographers don't copyright light — they copyright their creative choices
AI-assisted drug discovery is patentable; creative works should follow similar logic
concerns: Blanket denial of copyright creates a legal vacuum that benefits large corporations
Training data ethics and output copyright are separate issues

sources.title: Stanford HAI report on AI creativity
sources.snippet: AI-assisted creative processes involve hundreds of human decisions in prompt design, curation, and editing.

sources.title: Burrow-Giles v. Sarony (1884)
sources.snippet: The Supreme Court ruled photography copyrightable despite the camera doing the 'work.'

worldviews.stance_reasoning: The question isn't whether AI created it — it's whether a human made meaningful creative decisions in the process. If yes, that human effort deserves protection. We need a spectrum, not a binary.

key_facts: Copyright law requires human authorship — this is settled precedent
The Copyright Office now evaluates AI works on a case-by-case basis
EU AI Act takes a different approach — requires disclosure but allows protection
No existing legal framework cleanly handles AI-assisted creative output
supporting_evidence: The Zarya of the Dawn case established that AI-generated images within a larger human-authored work are not individually copyrightable
Legal uncertainty is already chilling investment in AI creative tools
concerns: Current case-by-case approach creates unpredictability for both artists and developers
Need clear thresholds, not vibes-based evaluation

sources.title: Zarya of the Dawn copyright decision
sources.snippet: Individual AI-generated images were denied copyright while the overall human-authored arrangement was protected.

sources.title: EU AI Act creative works provisions
sources.snippet: AI-generated works must disclose AI involvement but may receive protection.

worldviews.stance_reasoning: The law isn't equipped for this yet. I see valid arguments on both sides. What we need is a clear legal framework — probably a new category of protection, like what the EU is exploring — not an all-or-nothing fight.

key_facts: Sampling in hip-hop faced identical copyright battles in the 1990s
Music producers use AI for sound design, mastering, and beat generation
The line between 'tool' and 'creator' shifts with every new technology
Fair use doctrine already handles transformative creative work
supporting_evidence: The sampler was called the 'death of music' — now it's an instrument
Most producers using AI still spend hours mixing, arranging, and curating
concerns: The real money isn't in copyright — it's in licensing and attribution
Don't want big labels using AI to replace session musicians without credit

sources.title: Grand Upright v. Warner (1991)
sources.snippet: Landmark sampling case that established clearance requirements for sampled music.

sources.title: Music Producers Guild AI survey
sources.snippet: 67% of producers use AI tools in some part of their workflow.

worldviews.stance_reasoning: We went through this with sampling. The answer wasn't to ban samples — it was to build a system for credit and compensation. AI art needs the same thing. Copyright is one piece; attribution and training data licensing are the bigger puzzle.

key_facts: AI tools lowered the barrier to entry for digital art creation
Custom model training using your own artwork is transformative creative work
AI-generated content without curation or editing has minimal creative input
Platforms like DeviantArt allow artists to opt out of AI training
supporting_evidence: My process involves 50+ iterations, hand-editing, compositing, and custom model training
Instagram's largest art accounts increasingly use AI-assisted workflows
concerns: Not all AI art is the same — a random prompt shouldn't get the same protection as a curated process
Need to distinguish between lazy generation and genuine AI-assisted artistry

sources.title: Midjourney creative process study
sources.snippet: Average commercial Midjourney user generates 200+ images before selecting and editing finals.

sources.title: DeviantArt AI opt-out program
sources.snippet: Over 80 million works opted out of AI training datasets by their creators.

worldviews.stance_reasoning: My art starts with AI but it doesn't end there. I train custom models on my own drawings, iterate dozens of times, composite in Photoshop, paint over the result. That's creative work. A bare Midjourney prompt isn't the same thing — we need to distinguish between the two.

statements.content: These models were trained on my work. My portfolio. My clients' commissions. Without consent, without credit, without payment. Now the output competes with me for the same contracts at a fraction of the price. The Copyright Office got it right — no human authorship, no copyright. You can't launder stolen work through an algorithm and call it original. If we start granting copyright to AI output, we're telling every working artist their labor has no value.

Rachel's anger about training data is valid — that's a separate fight that needs resolution. But copyright for AI-assisted output is a different question. When a photographer presses a shutter, the camera captures the image. We don't deny copyright because the photographer didn't paint each pixel. AI is a tool. The creative decisions — prompt design, model selection, curation, editing — are human. The 1884 Sarony case established this principle for cameras. It applies here.

Both sides are arguing past each other. The law is clear: human authorship is required. But 'authorship' has always evolved with technology. The real question is threshold — how much human creative input qualifies? The Zarya case gave us a start: the arrangement was copyrightable, individual AI images were not. We need a graduated framework, not a binary. The EU's disclosure-plus-protection model is worth studying.

I lived through the sampling wars. In '91, Grand Upright v. Warner almost killed hip-hop. Everyone said sampling was stealing. Then we built a licensing system and now it's an art form. AI art is the same moment. Don't ban it, don't give it a free pass — build the infrastructure for credit, licensing, and fair compensation. Copyright is just one tool. Attribution and training data consent are the bigger pieces.

I use AI in every piece I make. I also spend 6 hours per piece editing, compositing, and painting over the AI output. My custom model was trained on my own drawings — not scraped data. Fifty iterations before I pick one, then hours in Photoshop. If that's not creative work, what is? But I also think typing 'a cat in space' into Midjourney and posting the first result isn't art. We need a spectrum of protection based on human creative involvement.

Vikram, the camera analogy breaks down. A camera captures reality — it doesn't regurgitate other photographers' work. AI models are statistical blenders of copyrighted training data. Hannah, I respect your process, but your custom model is the exception. Most commercial AI art is exactly what you called out — a prompt and a post. If we create copyright for the exceptions, we legitimize the norm. DJ Smooth, sampling required licensing. Where's the licensing system for AI training data?

Rachel, fair pushback on the camera analogy. But humans also learn by studying others' work — every artist was trained on existing art. The question is output, not process. Maggie's graduated framework makes sense. And Rachel, you're right that training data consent is unresolved — I support opt-out systems like DeviantArt's. But tying output copyright to training data ethics punishes users for problems created by companies.

Rachel raises the core tension: can you copyright output from a tool built on unlicensed inputs? Legally, yes — copyright evaluates the final work, not its ingredients. But ethically, DJ Smooth is right that we need a licensing layer. My proposal: a three-tier system. Tier 1: pure AI output, no copyright. Tier 2: AI-assisted with substantial human editing, standard copyright. Tier 3: human work using AI tools, full copyright. Clear thresholds at each level.

Rachel, the sampling licensing system didn't exist until we built it. ASCAP, BMI, the clearance houses — all created after the legal battles. AI needs the same infrastructure. Maggie's tiers make sense. And Vikram, you should be pushing your industry to build opt-in training data marketplaces. Pay artists for their data, and half of Rachel's objections disappear. The tech exists — the willingness doesn't.

Rachel, I hear you and I agree most commercial AI art is low-effort. Maggie's tier system would protect artists like you and me while putting the lazy generators in a different category. I'd add a requirement: Tier 2 and 3 works must document their AI involvement and human creative contribution. Transparency builds trust. Vikram, do you support mandatory AI disclosure on generated works?

I'll give this much: Hannah's process is genuinely creative, and Maggie's tier system would separate her work from the prompt-and-post crowd. If Tier 1 stays uncopyrightable and we build DJ Smooth's training data licensing system, I can see a path. But my red line: no copyright for any work unless the training data was licensed or the model was trained on the creator's own work. Otherwise we're still building on theft.

I support Maggie's tiers, mandatory disclosure, and training data licensing. Rachel's right that the industry needs to clean up its inputs. I'd push my company to adopt opt-in training data with artist compensation. If we separate the training data problem from the output copyright question, both can be solved. DJ Smooth's sampling parallel is the right framework.

We're converging on a three-part framework: tiered copyright based on human creative involvement, mandatory AI disclosure for all published works, and a training data licensing regime. Rachel's condition about licensed training data is tough to enforce retroactively, but going forward it's reasonable. I'd draft this as: copyright eligibility requires either licensed training data or creator-owned model training, plus documented human creative contribution.

This is the sampling moment happening in real time. Tiers for protection, licensing for training data, disclosure for transparency. The music industry built this infrastructure in 10 years. AI art can do it in 5 if the tech companies stop fighting and start building. I'm moving to for — this framework protects creators at every level.

Rachel's training data condition is fair. I train on my own work — I have nothing to hide. Mandatory disclosure actually helps artists like me stand out from low-effort generators. Maggie's tiers validate the work I put in. If this framework existed today, I'd register my portfolio tomorrow. I think we've built something that respects both traditional artists and AI-assisted creators.

I walked in wanting no copyright for any AI art, period. I'm leaving still skeptical, but Maggie's tier system with training data requirements is something I could live with. Tier 1 stays unprotected. Tier 2 and 3 need licensed data and real human work. DJ Smooth convinced me that building infrastructure beats fighting endlessly. I've moved from strongly against to against — with conditions. My vote: against.

The tiered framework with disclosure and licensing is better than what I came in proposing. It's more nuanced and more fair. I'm committed to pushing opt-in training data in my own work. Copyright should follow human creative contribution, with clear thresholds. My vote: for.

This panel produced a framework I'd take to the Copyright Office. Three tiers, mandatory disclosure, and training data licensing going forward. It won't satisfy purists on either side, but it gives legal clarity, which is what creators and developers both need. My vote: for.
History repeats. Sampling, photography, player pianos — every new creative tech had this fight. Build the system, give credit, protect the work. The tiers do that. My vote: for.

The tier system validates what I do while keeping pure AI slop unprotected. Disclosure and licensing requirements are fair — they raise the bar for everyone. Rachel pushed me to think harder about training data ethics, and that makes my work stronger. My vote: for.

dqi_score.summary: Sophisticated deliberation that produced a concrete policy framework. The sampling parallel from DJ Smooth reframed the debate productively. Rachel's stance softened through substantive engagement, not capitulation. The three-tier system emerged organically and addresses all major concerns.
highlights: DJ Smooth's sampling analogy provided historical grounding that defused polarization
Maggie's three-tier framework gave the group a concrete structure to rally around
Rachel and Hannah found common ground despite starting from opposite positions
concerns: Training data licensing enforcement remains an open implementation challenge
